reviews.summary: This paper studies path-dependent setup costs in sequential causal design through a fixed-skeleton orientation planning surrogate. The main contribution is a theoretical proposition characterizing when scalar switching penalties can change the optimal policy—only through feasibility changes or small additive-value gaps. The empirical evidence is intentionally narrow (one graph family, one seed, budget 14, oracle skeletons) and positioned as a pilot study. The authors transparently report that the posterior approximation fails audit thresholds and the learned-skeleton audit is unresolved.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted 8+ distinct online searches to verify novelty: (1) Exact title search yields no existing paper. (2) Core technique searches ('path-dependent setup costs causal intervention design', 'scalar switching penalty finite-horizon budgeted') reveal no directly overlapping work. (3) Key related works were verified to exist: Kocaoglu et al. 2017, Ghassami et al. 2018, Zheng et al. 2020 (NeurIPS), Pricopie et al. 2024 (GECCO), Agrawal et al. 2019, Annadani et al. 2023, Zhang et al. 2022/2023. (4) Recent NeurIPS 2023-2024 proceedings show active work in causal experimental design (GO-CBED, multi-target causal BED) but none addressing switching costs in orientation planning. The specific combination of scalar switching penalties with finite-horizon budgeted causal orientation planning appears novel. The theoretical proposition cleanly isolates the mechanisms (feasibility changes, small value gaps) through which switching costs can matter.
strengths: The theoretical proposition (Proposition 1) provides a clean, interpretable characterization of when scalar switching penalties can affect optimal policies.
The paper is exceptionally honest about its limitations—the authors explicitly state the evidence is narrow, the posterior audit fails, and the correct reading is a 'theorem-plus-pilot note'.
All claimed numbers in tables exactly match the results.json artifacts (verified: main benchmark AUEC 5.1701371920588265e-06, exact validation v_b 2.3051295810162964, etc.).
Experiment code exists and implements what the paper describes—planners.py shows the switching-aware planning logic.
Reference integrity is strong—all 12 citations are real, verifiable publications with correct titles, authors, and venues.

The positioning relative to prior work is nuanced and accurate, distinguishing from additive-cost causal design (Kocaoglu et al., Ghassami et al.) and sequential causal BED (Annadani et al., Zhang et al. 2025).
weaknesses: The empirical scope is extremely limited: one graph family (Erdős-Rényi), one seed (11), one budget (14), oracle skeletons, and only two switching regimes (S2, S3). This provides minimal evidence for generalizability.

The main pilot results show complete invariance—all three planners tie at AUEC ≈ 5.17×10^-6, suggesting switching costs don't matter in this regime, but the paper doesn't demonstrate a regime where they do matter.
The posterior audit fails both predefined thresholds (TV error 0.186 vs threshold 0.05; first-action agreement 0.0 vs threshold 0.85), undermining confidence in approximate-posterior conclusions.
The ablation artifact pools multiple internal tags ('proposed', 'ablation_c', 'ablation_d') into shared rows, preventing clean component-level analysis.
No statistical significance tests are reported for the (minimal) observed differences between methods.
The theoretical result is limited to scalar switching penalties; the asymmetric regime S3 (which violates this) is retained only as a 'practical stress test' without theoretical grounding.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper makes a modest but legitimate contribution: a clean theoretical characterization of scalar switching penalties in finite-horizon budgeted planning, backed by an auditable (if limited) pilot. The authors should be commended for their transparency about limitations. For revision, I recommend: (1) Expand the empirical scope to include at least 3-5 seeds and demonstrate a regime where switching costs actually change the optimal policy (not just invariant cases). (2) Address the posterior approximation issues or further emphasize that approximate-posterior conclusions are qualitative only. (3) Unpool the ablation results to enable clean per-component analysis. (4) Add discussion of why the S3 asymmetric regime matters despite violating the scalar-penalty assumption. (5) Clarify whether the invariance results are due to problem structure (small additive-value gaps never occur) or algorithm limitations (planners too myopic to find switching-advantageous sequences). The theoretical contribution alone may warrant acceptance if the experimental scope limitations are explicitly framed as a pilot study, but stronger empirical validation would strengthen the paper considerably.
questions_for_authors: Can you demonstrate any experimental regime where the switching-aware planner actually outperforms the additive-cost planner? The current results show only ties.
Is the invariance in the main pilot due to the problem structure (no small additive-value gaps exist) or due to planner limitations (horizon-2 search too shallow)?
Why include the asymmetric S3 regime when it violates the scalar-penalty assumption underlying Proposition 1?
How sensitive are the results to the posterior approximation quality given the TV error of 0.186?
What would be required to scale this to learned (non-oracle) skeletons, given the unresolved skeleton audit?
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity: VERIFIED. All numbers in paper match results.json exactly (checked: Table 1 AUEC values, Table 2 v_b and AUEC values, Table 3 audit metrics). Code exists: VERIFIED. Experiment code in exp/shared/planners.py implements the described methods including switching-aware planning. Logs show actual training runs. Reference integrity: VERIFIED. All 12 citations are real publications—checked Kocaoglu et al. 2017, Ghassami et al. 2018, Zheng et al. 2020, Pricopie et al. 2024, Agrawal et al. 2019, Annadani et al. 2023, Zhang et al. 2022/2023. Figures generated from results: VERIFIED. The paper explicitly ties every figure to specific JSON artifacts. Limitations disclosed: YES. The authors transparently report narrow scope, failed audits, and pooled ablations.

reviews.summary: The paper studies a narrow surrogate for sequential causal design with path-dependent family-switching costs: given a fixed skeleton, it maintains a posterior over acyclic orientations and selects hard or soft singleton interventions under a budget to reduce terminal orientation entropy. Its contributions are a simple finite-horizon characterization for scalar switching penalties and a small pilot study showing mostly invariance cases, with an exact d=8 slice indicating that shallow search quality can matter more than explicit switching-cost modeling.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks: exact-title searches on Google Scholar and Semantic Scholar did not surface an existing paper with this exact title, but multiple close neighbors substantially narrow the claim. The most relevant prior work includes ABCD-Strategy: Budgeted Experimental Design for Targeted Causal Structure Discovery (https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.10347), Budgeted Experiment Design for Causal Structure Learning (ICML 2018), Cost-Optimal Learning of Causal Graphs (ICML 2017), Experimental Design for Cost-Aware Learning of Causal Graphs (https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.11867), Adaptive Online Experimental Design for Causal Discovery (https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.11548), Differentiable Multi-Target Causal Bayesian Experimental Design (https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.10607), Goal-Oriented Sequential Bayesian Experimental Design for Causal Learning (https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.07359), and Sequential Bayesian Experimental Design with Variable Cost Structure (NeurIPS 2020). I also checked recent NeurIPS-style proceedings searches for adjacent sequential/causal experimental-design work. I did not find a clearly identical prior paper on switching/setup costs specifically for causal design, so the theorem-plus-cost-model angle may be new, but the overall contribution is incremental relative to substantial existing literature on budgeted, sequential, and cost-aware causal experimental design. Novelty is therefore limited rather than absent.
strengths: The manuscript is unusually honest about scope and limitations; it repeatedly frames the work as a theorem-plus-pilot note rather than overselling the empirical evidence.
The structural proposition for scalar switching penalties is clean and easy to understand, and the paper clearly distinguishes the symmetric-theory case from the asymmetric S3 stress test.
The main tables in paper.tex match the released group summaries in exp/main_benchmark/results.json, exp/exact_validation/results.json, exp/audits/results.json, and exp/ablations/results.json.
Source code is present and mostly consistent with the stated surrogate setup: fixed skeleton, posterior over orientations/consistent DAGs, hard/soft intervention families, and budgeted planners.
The exact d=8 slice is more informative than the approximate main slice and usefully shows that a stronger search baseline can dominate both horizon-2 planners in S3.
weaknesses: The empirical evidence is extremely narrow: effectively one graph family, one seed, one main budget, oracle skeletons for the main and ablation slices, and only a tiny exact-validation slice.
The approximate posterior fails its own audit thresholds badly (TV error 0.1857, first-action agreement 0.0), so the approximate-posterior experimental conclusions are heavily confounded.

Experimental rigor is weak: no multi-seed statistics for the reported pilot, no meaningful uncertainty estimates in the paper tables, no significance testing for the reported comparisons, and the main slice is near-degenerate with all three planners tied at essentially zero remaining entropy.

The proposed switching-aware planner does not beat the matched additive horizon-2 baseline in the reported exact slice; the main empirical takeaway is mostly that switching often did not matter or that shallow search was insufficient.

Reproducibility/integrity is weakened by artifact inconsistency: exp/main_benchmark/results.json and exp/ablations/results.json advertise planned config values such as particle_count_main=96, t_max=8, top_k=5, and rollouts_main=16, while the actual per-run CSV rows show particle_count=16, t_max=2, top_k=1, and rollouts=1.

The workspace notes (especially exp/RERUN_BLOCKED.md) indicate the full repaired rerun was not completed and describe the current outputs as a narrowed pilot/fallback, which makes the artifact story unstable for a conference submission.
Related work is directionally adequate but still under-positioned given how much nearby work already exists on budgeted causal design, sequential causal BED, and variable-cost sequential design.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper should be tightened further around the only defensible claim: a narrow theoretical observation about scalar switching penalties in a fixed-skeleton orientation surrogate. To become competitive, the authors need a completed and internally consistent rerun with metadata that matches the actual executed settings, multiple seeds, non-degenerate regimes where switching materially changes decisions, and audits that either pass or are strong enough to isolate exactly where approximation error enters. The empirical section should then focus on fair matched comparisons between additive and switching-aware planning under those validated settings, with proper uncertainty estimates and a clearer separation between exact-posterior evidence and approximate-posterior trends. If that is infeasible, the work would be better positioned as a short workshop note or technical report centered on the proposition rather than as a full NeurIPS paper.
questions_for_authors: Why do exp/main_benchmark/results.json and exp/ablations/results.json record the larger planned configuration (e.g., particle_count_main=96, t_max=8, top_k=5, rollouts_main=16) while the actual per-run CSV rows show the lighter pilot settings (particle_count=16, t_max=2, top_k=1, rollouts=1)? Which settings should a reader trust?

Can you provide a completed rerun with multiple seeds and at least one clearly non-degenerate regime in which switching-aware planning improves over the matched additive horizon-2 baseline under validated posterior quality?
Given that the posterior audit fails the predefined thresholds, what empirical claims do you believe remain valid beyond the exact d=8 slice?
Do you have a stronger theoretical result for asymmetric switching matrices such as S3, or should all theorem-linked discussion be restricted to the scalar/symmetric case only?
Can you release figure-generation code or figure metadata that directly maps the current PDF figures to the current exp/*/results.json artifacts?
reviews.integrity_check: The paper’s reported table values are consistent with the released exp/*/results.json summaries, and experiment source code plus round-level CSV logs are present. However, I do not view the results as fully trustworthy for publication-grade evidence because the accessible logs are limited, the posterior audit fails, the skeleton audit is unresolved, and there is a material mismatch between the configuration metadata stored in results.json and the actual settings recorded in the per-run CSV artifacts. That is not outright fabrication, but it is a serious reproducibility/integrity concern and supports rejection.

reviews.summary: The paper studies whether path-dependent setup (switching) costs change the optimal policy in sequential causal intervention design under a fixed budget. The main theoretical contribution is a proposition showing that scalar switching penalties can only alter the optimizer via feasibility changes or small additive-value gaps. The empirical component is an extremely narrow pilot (single seed, single graph family, budget 14) where all methods tie in the main benchmark and the posterior audit fails its own predefined thresholds.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted 8+ online searches across Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, and arXiv. No paper with the exact title exists. The idea of switching/setup costs in sequential experimental design has been studied in Bayesian optimization (Pricopie et al. GECCO/PPSN 2024; IJCAI 2023 BO with switching costs) and variable-cost BED (Zheng et al. NeurIPS 2020), both cited by the authors. The specific application to causal orientation planning is new but extremely narrow. The core proposition (Proposition 1) is near-tautological: it states that a higher-switch policy is ranked below a lower-switch policy iff its value advantage is less than its switching surcharge—this follows directly from the definition of the penalized objective J_lambda. The decomposition into feasibility and ranking-reversal mechanisms, while clean, adds minimal insight beyond restating the constrained optimization structure. The combination of switching costs with causal orientation planning is novel in topic but not in technique or insight.
strengths: Exceptional honesty about limitations: the paper explicitly states the posterior audit fails, the scope is a single seed/family/budget, and the manuscript is only a pilot note. This level of transparency is commendable.
All 12 references are verified as real, published papers with correct titles, authors, and venues. No hallucinated citations.
Numbers in all four paper tables (Tables 1-4) match the corresponding exp/*/results.json artifacts exactly, demonstrating careful artifact management.

Code is well-structured: exp/shared/ contains modular implementations of planners (planners.py), posterior maintenance (posterior.py), data generation (data.py), metrics (metrics.py), and a runner framework (runner.py). The implementation matches what the paper describes.
The problem formulation (Section 3.1) is clean and well-defined, with clear notation for the orientation-planning surrogate, action costs, and value functions.
weaknesses: Proposition 1 is near-trivial. Part (1) says J_lambda(pi_1) < J_lambda(pi_2) iff V_0(pi_1) - V_0(pi_2) < lambda*(N_sw(pi_1) - N_sw(pi_2)), which is just algebraic rearrangement of the definition J_lambda = V_0 - lambda*N_sw. Part (2) adds only the obvious observation that hard-budget feasibility can also change. This does not constitute a meaningful theoretical contribution for a top venue.

The main benchmark (Table 1) is completely uninformative: all three planners tie exactly with v_b = 0.0 and near-zero AUEC across both switching regimes. The paper admits 'remaining orientation entropy is already extremely close to zero,' meaning the experimental setup provides no discriminative power whatsoever.

Experimental scope is fatally narrow: one graph family (Erdos-Renyi), one seed (11), one budget (14), oracle skeletons only. No error bars, no confidence intervals from multiple seeds, no statistical tests. n=1 per method-regime cell in every table.

The posterior approximation fails both of its own predefined audit thresholds (TV error 0.186 vs threshold 0.05; first-action agreement 0.0 vs threshold 0.85). This means the approximate-posterior results cannot support any empirical claims.

The ablation artifact (Table 4) pools three internal tags (proposed, ablation_c, ablation_d) into a single row, making it impossible to isolate the contribution of individual components. The paper acknowledges this but provides no solution.

In the exact d=8 validation, the proposed H=2 switching-aware planner ties with the H=2 additive planner and is outperformed by both exact DP and the myopic rule in S3. The proposed method shows zero advantage over its ablation (ignoring switching costs).

The paper explicitly positions itself as 'not a submission-ready broad empirical benchmark' and 'a theorem-plus-pilot note.' By the authors' own assessment, this is below the threshold for a venue contribution.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper's greatest weakness is that it presents a trivial proposition paired with experiments that fail to discriminate between methods. To become publishable, it would need: (1) a substantially deeper theoretical analysis—e.g., characterizing when switching costs provably change the optimal policy in terms of graph structure, budget-to-cost ratios, or posterior concentration, rather than just restating the optimization definition; (2) experiments across multiple seeds (at minimum 5-10), multiple graph families, multiple budgets, and learned skeletons, with proper error bars and statistical significance tests; (3) a posterior approximation that actually passes its audit thresholds, so approximate-posterior results carry weight; (4) at least one experimental regime where the switching-aware planner demonstrably outperforms the additive planner, providing evidence that modeling switching costs matters in practice; (5) properly separated ablation rows so each component's contribution can be assessed. The current writing quality is good, and the artifact management is exemplary—these strengths should be preserved in a revision.
questions_for_authors: Can you construct or identify any experimental configuration where the switching-aware planner actually outperforms the additive planner? The current evidence shows exact ties in all cases, which undermines the motivation for the entire framework.
Why was only a single random seed used? Given that all results show n=1 per cell, how do you know these results are not artifacts of the specific graph instance?

The proposition applies only to scalar (symmetric) penalties but S3 is asymmetric. Why include S3 results when the proposition explicitly excludes asymmetric regimes? What theoretical statement, if any, covers S3?

The posterior audit shows TV error of 0.186 and zero first-action agreement with exact inference. Have you investigated why the approximate posterior is so poor? Is this a fundamental limitation of the particle count (96) or a bug in the posterior update?

What is the initial orientation entropy for the seed-11, d=15 Erdos-Renyi instance? The fact that v_b=0.0 for all methods suggests the skeleton may be trivially oriented from observational data alone.
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity is acceptable. All numbers in the paper's four tables match the corresponding exp/*/results.json artifacts exactly (verified for main benchmark AUEC values, exact validation v_b and AUEC values, and audit TV/agreement values). Source code exists in exp/shared/ (planners.py, posterior.py, data.py, runner.py, metrics.py, config.py) plus experiment scripts (run_all.py, run_focused_stage3.py, and per-experiment run.py files). The code implements the methods described in the paper. Execution logs exist (20 Codex sessions with stdout/stderr/events). Figures exist as PDFs in figures/ though with different filenames than what the generation scripts produce, suggesting a separate figure-generation step. The root results.json contains only schema metadata (column names and row counts) rather than actual data; the real data is in exp/*/results.json. No fabrication detected, but the experiments themselves are too narrow to be informative.

(2) Address the posterior approximation issues or further emphasize that approximate-posterior conclusions are qualitative only. (5) Clarify whether the invariance results are due to problem structure (small additive-value gaps never occur) or algorithm limitations (planners too myopic to find switching-advantageous sequences).
